_meta.license: Original illustrations — may be used freely.
_meta.notes: Short sermon/teaching illustrations keyed to passages in Proverbs. Each tells a brief story or analogy and ties back to the verse. Edit freely in TheTruth.

items.title: The Cracked Foundation
items.text: A builder once boasted that he could finish a house in half the time of his rivals. He skipped the soil tests and poured the slab on a Friday. Within two years, hairline cracks ran through every wall. Wisdom is the same: every chapter we try to build without the fear of the LORD develops fault lines we cannot see until the rain comes. The fear of the LORD is not the upstairs decoration; it is the slab everything else is poured on.

items.title: Digging for Silver
items.text: When silver was discovered in the Comstock Lode, men sold their farms and crossed continents to dig. They moved tons of rock for an ounce of metal. Solomon says wisdom is like that — but most of us treat it like loose change. Cry for it. Lift up your voice. Search as for hidden treasure. The promise is not that the LORD will hand it to the curious, but that He will be found by the desperate.

items.title: The Pilot's Instruments
items.text: A small-plane pilot in fog has two choices: trust his inner ear or trust his instruments. The inner ear lies. Pilots who trust it spiral into the ground every year. Trust the instruments and you live. 'Lean not on your own understanding' is not anti-intellectual; it is anti-suicidal. Your gut, in the fog of life, will fly you into a mountain. The Word is the instrument panel. Read it. Obey it. Land safely.

items.title: The First Slice
items.text: A grandmother used to cut her pie and slide the first piece onto a separate plate before anyone sat down. 'That one is the Lord's,' she'd say. The grandkids learned generosity not from a sermon but from a kitchen knife. Honor God with the first, not the leftover. The barn fills when the offering comes off the top.

items.title: The Watershed
items.text: On the Continental Divide a single raindrop can fall on either side of a ridge a few feet wide; one side flows to the Pacific, the other to the Atlantic. The heart is that ridge. What you guard there determines which ocean your whole life flows into. Don't guard the words and ignore the watershed.

items.title: Your Own Well
items.text: In ancient villages every family dug its own well. To drink from a neighbor's cistern was to confess your own had run dry — a public shame. Solomon uses that picture for sexual fidelity. The world says variety is freedom; God says faithfulness is the deepest pleasure. Tend your own well, and rejoice in the wife of your youth.

items.title: The Ant Has No Foreman
items.text: Watch an anthill for ten minutes. No supervisor blows a whistle. No clipboard. Each ant simply works. Solomon does not send the sluggard to a productivity coach — he sends him to a bug. The lesson is not how to do more; it is how to need no one to make you do anything. Initiative is wisdom in work clothes.

items.title: The Lattice Window
items.text: Solomon doesn't preach the dangers of adultery from a pulpit; he opens a window and tells you to look. A young man crosses the street near her house at twilight. He's not yet sinning — he's positioning. Most falls are not slips; they are walks taken with eyes half-closed. Wisdom is choosing a different street long before the lattice window appears.

items.title: She Stands at the Crossroads
items.text: Wisdom is pictured not as a hermit in a cave but as a woman shouting at the busiest intersection in town. The problem is not that wisdom is hard to find; it is that we walk past her with earbuds in. She is loud. She is public. She is calling your name today. The question is whether you'll pull the headphones out.

items.title: Two Banquets
items.text: Imagine walking down a street with two open doors. From one comes the smell of slow-roasted lamb — Wisdom has set her table. From the other, perfume and laughter — Folly is hosting too. Both call. Both promise satisfaction. One feeds you for a lifetime; the other leaves bones in the basement. Every day you choose a doorway. The smell tells the truth before you taste.

items.title: Two Headstones
items.text: Walk through any old cemetery. Some graves are tended every spring; others swallowed by weeds. The difference rarely tracks with wealth or fame. The memory of the righteous is blessed because it bears fruit beyond the casket — a grandchild still quoting their faith, a neighbor still telling their kindness. Build a name worth tending.

items.title: The Thumb on the Scale
items.text: A merchant once kept two sets of weights — heavy ones for buying, light ones for selling. He grew rich and died respected. But Proverbs says the LORD weighs the scales themselves. Heaven is not fooled by clever margins. Whether you sell wheat, hours, or words, the scale you use on others is the scale by which you'll be measured.

items.title: Words Like Surgery
items.text: There is a difference between a butcher's cleaver and a surgeon's scalpel. Both cut, but one heals. Many people speak the truth like a butcher — accurate, efficient, and bloody. The wise speak the same truth like a surgeon: precise, careful, with healing in view. Sharpness is not the issue; aim is.

items.title: The Five You Walk With
items.text: It has been said you become the average of the five people you spend the most time with. Solomon said it first, and harder: walk with the wise and become wise; companion of fools suffers harm. Audit your circle. Not to judge them — to know what you are slowly turning into.

items.title: The Smile in the Photograph
items.text: A pastor visited a family for Christmas dinner. Everyone smiled for the photo. Three months later the marriage was over. Even in laughter the heart may ache. Don't envy other people's highlight reels; pray for them. And don't pretend yourself — take the ache to the One who reads hearts past the smile.

items.title: Lowering the Voice
items.text: A husband and wife realized that every fight in their marriage escalated when one of them raised their voice. So they made a rule: when emotions rise, voices drop. Within a month, the fights were gone. Not the disagreements — the fights. A soft answer is not weakness; it is the off-ramp God gives you to take before the wreck.

items.title: The Detour
items.text: Driving through Tennessee, a family hit a closed bridge they had no idea about. The detour added two hours — and a chance encounter with a stranded driver who later became a lifelong friend. The heart plans the route; the LORD lays the road. Hold your plans like maps, not like demands. The detour is sometimes the destination.

items.title: The 2 A.M. Phone Call
items.text: Everyone has friends for sunny days. The test of a friend is who answers the phone at 2 A.M. when the diagnosis comes back, when the marriage cracks, when the child is in the ER. A brother is born for adversity. Be that friend long before adversity comes — you will not be conscripted then if you have not enlisted now.

items.title: The Half-Read Email
items.text: A manager once fired an employee over an email she'd only half read. The bottom paragraph contained the explanation. She apologized for a year. Folly answers what it has not heard. Wisdom listens to the bottom paragraph before reaching for the keyboard. Speed is not the same as clarity.

items.title: The Wedding Guest List
items.text: Wealth multiplies friends; poverty audits them. A man who lost his business in 2008 said the most painful surprise was who stopped calling. The fewer who remained were treasure. If God ever lets your barns shrink, thank Him for the friend list He clarifies. The poor man's friends are real ones.

items.title: The Mocker in the Glass
items.text: Alcohol does not introduce sins; it removes the brakes from the ones already there. Solomon does not call wine immoral — he calls it a mocker. It will laugh at you while it lowers your guard. Whoever is led astray by it is not wise. Know what is in the glass, and know what is in your heart, before you mix the two.

items.title: The Channeled River
items.text: Farmers in dry country dig channels and the river flows where they direct it. To us a king's heart looks like a wild river — unpredictable, powerful, dangerous. To God it is a channel He is digging. Pray for your leaders not because they are good, but because the One who turns the river is.

items.title: The Bent Sapling
items.text: An arborist trains a young tree by tying it to a stake while the trunk is still pliable. Wait too long and the lean is permanent. The years of training a child are short. Use them. Not to produce a clone, but to set the trunk — so when storms come, the tree leans toward the light.

items.title: The Reference Letter
items.text: Years after a job is done, a reference letter is still being read. A good name is the longest reference letter you will ever write — written one decision at a time, signed by your habits. Riches buy a house; a good name opens doors no key fits.

items.title: The Morning After
items.text: Solomon writes the most vivid anti-drinking passage in Scripture not from outside the bar but inside the headache. He describes the bruises you don't remember getting and the man who wakes up saying, 'When shall I awake? I will seek another drink.' Sin always edits its trailer; God shows you the whole movie.

items.title: Seven Falls
items.text: A toddler learning to walk falls dozens of times in an hour. We do not call her a failure; we call her a walker. The righteous fall seven times and rise. The defining mark is not the fall — it is the rise. Stay down and you have made the fall final. Get up and the fall becomes a step.

items.title: Bread for the Enemy
items.text: A woman whose neighbor had spread rumors about her baked the neighbor a loaf of bread every Saturday for a year. By Christmas the neighbor was weeping on her doorstep. Heap coals of fire — not to burn, but to soften. Kindness is the only weapon hatred has no defense against.

items.title: Starve the Fire
items.text: A campfire dies the moment you stop feeding it. Gossip is the same. Walk into a room where it is burning and refuse to add a stick — the conversation will fizzle within minutes. Where there is no whisperer, contention quiets. You don't have to put the fire out. Just stop feeding it.

items.title: Sparks in the Dark
items.text: Two blades pressed against each other throw sparks. It looks like damage but is actually edge. We tend to think a sharpening friend is a flattering one; usually it is the one who asks the question we were avoiding. Find the friend who throws sparks, not the one who only hands you compliments.

items.title: Know Your Flock
items.text: A shepherd who manages from the porch loses sheep. Whether the flock is your children, your team, or your church, knowing the state means walking among them, learning names, noticing limps. Riches are not forever; presence is the dividend that compounds.

items.title: The Hidden Crack
items.text: An engineer once tried to hide a hairline crack in a beam with paint. The beam held — until it didn't. Hidden sin is exactly that crack. Cover it and you may pass the inspection of men, but the load is coming. He who confesses and forsakes finds mercy. The painted-over beam finds the hospital.

items.title: Without a Map
items.text: A hiker without a map will walk in circles for miles, certain he is making progress. Without revelation, people do the same with their lives. Energy without direction is just sweat. Vision — the Word of God read, believed, and obeyed — is what tells you whether you are climbing toward the summit or wandering further from camp.

items.title: Neither Too Much Nor Too Little
items.text: Agur prays the most underrated prayer in Scripture: give me neither poverty nor riches. Too rich, I'll forget You; too poor, I'll steal. Most people pray for one extreme and ask God to bless it. Agur asks for the boring middle — daily bread — because the middle is where worship survives.

items.title: The Quiet Empire
items.text: Proverbs 31 is not a checklist that crushes wives — it is the portrait a son draws of his mother because she taught him how to see. She is industrious, generous, fearless about tomorrow, and her secret is verse 30: she fears the LORD. Charm fades and beauty fades; reverence builds quiet empires.

items.title: The Last Step
items.text: Climbers say most accidents happen on the descent, not the summit — because by then guard is down. Pride is the descent that follows every accomplishment. The moment you congratulate yourself for not slipping is usually the moment you slip. Walk humbly all the way down the mountain.

items.title: Stronger Than the General
items.text: History remembers the generals who took cities. God remembers the man who took his own temper. Conquering a wall is one battle; conquering yourself is a thousand. Slow to anger is not slow-witted — it is heavyweight strength.

items.title: The Hinge
items.text: A door hinges all day and goes nowhere. The sluggard turns on his bed all night and wakes exhausted. Motion is not progress. Rolling over is not getting up. The first sermon Solomon preaches the lazy is simply: stand up.

items.title: Wings Already on the Bird
items.text: Look at your bank balance long enough and it will sprout wings. Solomon already saw this in 950 B.C. Wealth is a bird, not a building. Build with it; don't worship it. The man who treats his money as if it might fly tomorrow is rarely surprised when it does.

items.title: The Soft Shackle
items.text: Debt is the only chain you put on yourself with a smile. The borrower is servant to the lender — a soft shackle, but a shackle nonetheless. Freedom is rarely as expensive as the bondage we choose to avoid the discomfort of waiting.

items.title: The Loving No
items.text: A child runs toward a busy street; a parent shouts and grabs. Love said no. Discipline is not the absence of love — it is love refusing to lie about what is dangerous. He who spares the rod hates his son means he loves his comfort more than the child's life.

items.title: Coals in Your Lap
items.text: Can a man scoop fire into his lap and his clothes not burn? You read it and laugh — of course not. Then we open the laptop, the app, the secret tab, and assume tonight will be the exception. Solomon's question is rhetorical for a reason. Fire is not selective. Stop scooping.

items.title: Honey in the Hospital
items.text: Researchers have used honey for millennia on stubborn wounds; it heals what antibiotics cannot reach. Solomon noticed first: pleasant words are honeycomb — sweet to the soul, healing to the bones. Most people in your life are walking around with wounds. Bring honey to the hospital today.

items.title: Bold as a Lion
items.text: The wicked flee when no one pursues — every footstep behind them sounds like a sheriff. The righteous are bold as a lion because their conscience is clean. Boldness is not bravado; it is the natural posture of the cleared heart. Confess what needs confessing and you will not need to flee anymore.

items.title: The Cliff at the End of the Road
items.text: Some roads look right for the first ninety miles and end at a cliff. The traveler doesn't know until the last mile, when there is no room to turn around. There is a way that seems right to a man, but its end is death. The wise check the end of the road before they choose it; fools admire the pavement.

items.title: Who You Can Correct
items.text: Try to correct a mocker and you'll get hated. Correct a wise man and he'll thank you and grow. The simplest test of whether someone is wise: how do they receive a hard word? The same test works on you. The next correction you receive will tell you which group you're in.

items.title: The Poor Are Not the Punchline
items.text: Whoever mocks the poor mocks the One who made them. The man on the corner with the cardboard sign was knit together in a womb the same way you were. You may not know his story, but the LORD does. And the LORD takes mockery of His image personally.

items.title: The Friend Who Stings
items.text: Iodine on a cut stings; honey from a flatterer feels good. One heals; one hides infection. Faithful are the wounds of a friend. If no one in your life is allowed to wound you, you are surrounded by enemies' kisses and don't know it.

items.title: The Watered Soul
items.text: A farmer who hoards seed has nothing to plant. A man who waters others is watered himself. Generosity does not make you smaller; clutching does. The hand that opens is the hand that fills.

items.title: Inside the Walls
items.text: In ancient cities, when raiders crested the hill, the bell rang and everyone ran for the watchtower. Once the gate slammed shut, the arrows outside no longer mattered. The Name of the LORD is that tower. Most of us pace the wall arguing with the arrows; the wise are already inside.

items.title: Bringing Your Own Wood
items.text: Some people don't find arguments — they import them. As coals are to burning coals and wood to fire, so a contentious man kindles strife. Notice who walks into rooms and the temperature rises. Notice whether you are that person.

items.title: The Camera Always On
items.text: We behave better when we know we're being recorded. Solomon's news flash: you always are. The eyes of the LORD are in every place, beholding the evil and the good. This is not surveillance to dread; it is the steady gaze of a Father who sees what no one else applauds.

items.title: Don't Open the Letter
items.text: A man received a private letter, then read its contents aloud at the next dinner party. He was never trusted with another. Solomon: argue your case with your neighbor, but do not disclose another's secret. Some letters are not yours to read aloud, even if they came addressed to you.

items.title: Out of Season
items.text: Snow in summer would ruin the harvest. So is honor undeserved — it ruins the fool by confirming what should have been corrected. Be careful what you praise. Misplaced applause builds monuments to folly that the next generation has to tear down.

items.title: The Trap That Compliments You
items.text: The fear of man brings a snare. The trap looks like a compliment: 'What will they think? Will they still like me?' Every step you take to manage someone else's opinion is a step into a cage you decorated yourself. Trust in the LORD is the only key.

items.title: Don't Look at the Glass
items.text: Solomon doesn't say 'do not drink' first — he says 'do not look.' Sin enters through the eye long before it enters through the lip. The battle for sobriety, purity, contentment, and peace is fought at the level of the gaze. Look away early; pour out late.

items.title: Hidden on Purpose
items.text: Children love hide-and-seek not because they fear being found but because the seeking is the joy. It is the glory of God to conceal a matter; the glory of kings to search it out. The mysteries of Scripture are not God being stingy; they are God inviting you to play.

items.title: The Gentle Tongue Breaks Bones
items.text: Iron does not break bone; pressure over time breaks bone. A loud rant changes few minds; a soft, persistent appeal changes hearts and even rulers. By long forbearing is a prince persuaded, and a soft tongue breaks the bone. Be soft. Be steady. Be there tomorrow.

items.title: Even a Fool
items.text: Even a fool, when he holds his peace, is counted wise. Silence has saved more reputations than eloquence ever earned. If you cannot think of the right thing to say, the wisest thing is usually to say nothing at all and let the room conclude you are deeper than you are.

items.title: When the Lights Go Out
items.text: When the stage lights are on, the room is full. Cut the power and watch how many remain. Wealth multiplies friends; the rich have many. The friends who stay when the lights go out are the only ones whose names are worth remembering.

items.title: A Feast in Plain Bread
items.text: Two men sat at the same table eating the same bread. One ate it as a chore, one ate it as a feast. The merry heart has a continual feast — not because the menu changed, but because the heart did. Joy is mostly a posture, not a portion.

items.title: The Medicine of Laughter
items.text: Hospitals now run laughter therapy units; physicians prescribe joy. A merry heart does good like a medicine, but a broken spirit drieth the bones. The bones are listening to the spirit. Feed your spirit hope and your skeleton will hold.

items.title: What the Perfume Cannot Cover
items.text: In the ancient world, expensive oil announced you a block before you arrived. Yet Solomon insists a good name is better than precious oil. Cologne fades by lunchtime; reputation outlives the casket. Spend more on the name; less on the bottle.

items.title: Better the Crust
items.text: Better is a dry morsel and quietness than a house full of feasting with strife. A peaceful kitchen with leftovers beats a Thanksgiving table where everyone is trying not to cry. Build peace at home before you build the menu.

items.title: On the Roof
items.text: Better to dwell in a corner of the housetop than with a brawling woman in a wide house. The verse cuts both ways: it warns wives — and it warns the husbands who drove their wives to brawl. Don't make people choose the roof to escape you.

items.title: Don't Withhold Today's Good
items.text: Withhold not good from them to whom it is due, when it is in the power of thine hand to do it. The favor you owe is not made nobler by the wait. Pay the worker, return the call, send the thank-you, fix the wrong — today, while it is still in your hand.

items.title: Take a Different Street
items.text: Enter not into the path of the wicked. Avoid it, pass not by it, turn from it, and pass away. Five verbs because Solomon knows your feet. The way out of temptation is rarely heroic resistance at the door; it is choosing a different street weeks earlier.

items.title: The Free Class You're Skipping
items.text: Wisdom cries in the street, in the chief place of concourse — free admission, no tuition. Most of us pay thousands for advice we already heard once and ignored. The classroom isn't behind a velvet rope; it's at the corner of every conversation. Listen.

items.title: The Crown of White Hair
items.text: The hoary head is a crown of glory if it be found in the way of righteousness. A culture that worships youth misses the wisdom of survivors. Find a senior saint this week and ask one question: 'What do you wish you had known at my age?' Take notes.

items.title: Pounded but Unchanged
items.text: Though thou shouldest bray a fool in a mortar with a pestle, yet will not his foolishness depart from him. Some lessons no consequence can teach. Don't waste your life trying to grind people into wisdom. Pray for them. Live wisely yourself. Sometimes the only sermon they will hear is your peace.

items.title: Two Things Before I Die
items.text: Agur asks for only two things: remove far from me vanity and lies, and give me neither poverty nor riches. The shortest will, the wisest prayer. Most of our death-bed regrets center on those two requests we never made.

items.title: Small but Wise
items.text: Four creatures are little upon the earth but exceeding wise: ants, conies, locusts, spiders. None has size, status, or strength — only foresight, refuge, order, and persistence. You don't need to be great to be wise. You only need the right four habits.

items.title: When the Earth Trembles
items.text: Agur lists what topples a society: a servant who becomes king, a fool with a full belly, a hated woman married, a maid who supplants her mistress. The common thread is power without character. Pray for leaders to be ready before they rise.

items.title: Cold Drink at Harvest
items.text: As the cold of snow in the time of harvest, so is a faithful messenger to them that send him; for he refresheth the soul of his masters. Do what you said you'd do, when you said you'd do it. Reliability is iced tea on a 100-degree day. Be that to your boss, your spouse, your church.

items.title: The Counsel of a Friend
items.text: Ointment and perfume rejoice the heart; so doth the sweetness of a man's friend by hearty counsel. Some advice we pay therapists for could have been gotten over coffee with a friend who fears God. Don't outsource your soul. Cultivate counselors in your circle.

items.title: Where No Counsel Is
items.text: Where no counsel is, the people fall; but in the multitude of counselors there is safety. Don't make the biggest decision of the decade alone in the shower. Get five trusted voices in the room. The cost of pride is paid by the next decade.

items.title: Many Will Boast
items.text: Most men will proclaim every one his own goodness; but a faithful man who can find? Talk is cheap; faithfulness is rare. Be the friend who shows up at the funeral, the elder who keeps the secret, the husband who comes home. The world is starving for found ones.

items.title: Bruises That Cleanse
items.text: The blueness of a wound cleanseth away evil; so do stripes the inward parts of the belly. Sometimes God uses the consequences of sin to purge what no sermon could reach. The bruise is not God's anger — it is His scrubbing. Don't waste the wound by resenting it.

items.title: The Eye That Despises
items.text: The eye that mocketh at his father, and despiseth to obey his mother, the ravens of the valley shall pick it out. Strong language for a culture that smirks at parents. The way you treat the ones who raised you is a leading indicator of how you'll treat the God who made you.

items.title: He That Hasteth
items.text: Without knowledge the soul is not good; and he that hasteth with his feet sinneth. The fastest decision is rarely the best decision. Slow down enough to know what you're doing. Speed is not the same as faith; sometimes it is just panic in a hurry.

items.title: The Discretion to Defer
items.text: The discretion of a man defereth his anger; and it is his glory to pass over a transgression. Not every offense deserves a response. Letting it go is not weakness; it is the glory of strength under control. Choose which hills to die on — most are not worth the climb.

items.title: Built by Wisdom
items.text: Through wisdom is a house builded, by understanding it is established, and by knowledge shall the chambers be filled with all precious and pleasant riches. The architecture of a home is not square footage — it is wisdom in the corners. Knowledge furnishes rooms money cannot buy.

items.title: Don't Cheer the Stretcher
items.text: Rejoice not when thine enemy falleth, lest the LORD see it, and it displease Him. The temptation when an opponent stumbles is to laugh on the inside. God notices the inside. Pray for the fallen, even when the fallen wronged you. Heaven keeps a different scoreboard.

items.title: The Polluted Well
items.text: A righteous man falling down before the wicked is as a troubled fountain, and a corrupt spring. When godly people compromise publicly, the whole town's water gets dirty. Stand for what is right — not for your sake, but for everyone downstream of you.

items.title: A Jewel in a Snout
items.text: As a jewel of gold in a swine's snout, so is a fair woman without discretion. The image is jarring on purpose. Beauty without character is wasted treasure. Marry for character; train your sons to look for it; train your daughters to develop it.

items.title: What Children Make of Parents
items.text: A wise son maketh a glad father, but a foolish son is the heaviness of his mother. Long after report cards, parents read the deeper transcript of how their grown children live. Make your parents glad while you still have time — it is the easiest gift to give and the one most often delayed.

items.title: Money That Cannot Save
items.text: Treasures of wickedness profit nothing, but righteousness delivereth from death. Some people make a fortune and lose their soul; some lose their fortune and find their soul. At the end, righteousness writes the only check the grave will cash.

items.title: What Love Covers
items.text: Hatred stirreth up strifes, but love covereth all sins. To cover is not to deny — it is to refuse to broadcast. Love handles a brother's failure with a blanket, not a megaphone. The next failure you witness will reveal which you reach for first.

items.title: The Slack Hand
items.text: He becometh poor that dealeth with a slack hand, but the hand of the diligent maketh rich. Wealth is rarely a single deal; it is a thousand small acts of follow-through. The slack hand drops opportunities the diligent hand quietly catches.

items.title: Without Added Sorrow
items.text: The blessing of the LORD, it maketh rich, and He addeth no sorrow with it. Wealth gained by compromise comes with a sorrow rider you don't read until it's too late. Wealth from God arrives clean. Pray for the kind whose receipt has no fine print.

items.title: The Substituted Trouble
items.text: The righteous is delivered out of trouble, and the wicked cometh in his stead. There are deliverances we never see — trains we missed, deals that fell through, doors that didn't open. Years later we say 'thank You.' Often, we never know what God spared us from. Trust Him in the no's.

items.title: Trees That Grow People
items.text: The fruit of the righteous is a tree of life, and he that winneth souls is wise. The wisest investment is not a portfolio — it is a person. Souls won outlast bull markets. Plant a tree this year that grows people, not just income.

items.title: The Lip That Lasts
items.text: The lip of truth shall be established forever, but a lying tongue is but for a moment. Lies have an expiration date — short shelf life, public exposure, embarrassing aftermath. Truth, even unpopular truth, outlives every news cycle. Build with words that last.

items.title: When the Tree Comes In
items.text: Hope deferred maketh the heart sick, but when the desire cometh, it is a tree of life. The waiting room is the hardest seat in the kingdom. But God plants oaks in the soil of long patience. Don't pull up the seed because the sprout is slow.

items.title: The Builder and the Wrecker
items.text: Every wise woman buildeth her house, but the foolish plucketh it down with her hands. Houses are not undone by storms half so often as by their occupants. The same hands can lay bricks or pull them; choose every day which the hands will do.

items.title: Mutual Disgust
items.text: An unjust man is an abomination to the just; and he that is upright in the way is an abomination to the wicked. Don't be surprised when the world finds you offensive. The light disturbs the dark on principle. Quiet faithfulness is its own provocation.

items.title: A Place of Refuge for Children
items.text: In the fear of the LORD is strong confidence, and His children shall have a place of refuge. The greatest legacy you can leave your children isn't a trust fund — it is a Father they know they can run to long after you're gone. Build a home that points them upward.
